content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob have got an integer NNN. They decide to play a game. Alice and Bob make alternating moves: Alice makes the first move, Bob makes the second move, Alice makes the third one, and so on. The game continues until NNN becomes 000. The player who is unable to make a move loses.

During each turn a player can make one of the following moves:



Choose an integer XXX such that XXX can be expressed as 2Y2^Y2Y, Y≥1Y \geq 1Y≥1. The chosen XXX must also be a factor of NNN. After choosing an integer XXX which satisfies the mentioned criteria, the player will divide NNN by XXX.




If Move 111 is not possible , the player will subtract 111 from NNN.



Predict the winner of the game if the game is played optimally by both the players.


Input Format


The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.





Output Format
For each test case, print "Alice" if Alice wins the game else print "Bob". Print the output without quotes.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "Alice" if Alice wins the game when both play optimally, otherwise output "Bob".

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=1: only move is subtract 1 → 0, opponent cannot move, so Alice wins. N=2: possible moves lead to N=1 which is winning for the next player, so Alice loses.

3 → subtract 1 → 2 (losing) so Alice wins. 4 → divide by 2 → 2 (losing) so Alice wins. 5 → subtract 1 → 4 (winning) so Alice loses. 6 → divide by 2 → 3 (winning for opponent) but subtract 1 → 5 (losing) gives Alice a winning move.
8: divide by 8 → 1 (winning for opponent) but divide by 4 → 2 (losing) gives a win. 10: divide by 2 → 5 (losing) gives a win. 2 is the unique losing start.

solution.approach:
The game is impartial. A position N is losing if the player to move has no move to a losing position. For odd N > 3 the only possible move is N-1, which is even and >=4, a winning position (proved below). Hence all odd N > 3 are losing. N=1 and N=3 are winning because N-1 = 0 or 2, both losing. For even N:
- If N = 2, the only moves lead to N=1 (winning), so N=2 is losing.
- If N >= 4, the player can subtract 1, reaching an odd number N-1 >=3. When N=4, N-1=3 is winning, but the player can instead divide by 2 to reach N=2 (losing). For any even N >=6, N-1 is an odd number >3, which we already know is losing. Therefore every even N >=4 is winning. Consequently the only losing starting position is N = 2. The optimal strategy is simply: if N == 2, Alice loses; otherwise Alice wins.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
